metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add ticket 2bbd as a formal depends_on dependency (so the close gate exists when this ticket's ACs are verified) or move AC #2 to the 2bbd ticket and remove the 'close story' migration language from this plan's Implementation section.
evidence: Plan ticket comment: 'Moved exact close wiring to 2bbd: transition_close.close_ticket calls the reusable close profile under require_plan_review_for_close before completion verification'
Ticket deps field: only acaf-bb4d-9eba-470c is listed; 2bbd is absent
require_plan_review_for_close: grep returns 0 matches in src/
src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py not in file_impact
src/rebar/_commands/gates.py not in file_impact
findings.finding: AC #2 ('Enabled pin drift invalidates the close-profile consumer when that gate is active') and the Implementation's 'close story' migration both require a require_plan_review_for_close gate and close-path wiring in transition_close.py. The plan's own ticket comment says 'Moved exact close wiring to 2bbd,' but ticket 2bbd is not in this ticket's deps list, require_plan_review_for_close does not exist in _config_schema.py, and transition_close.py is not in file_impact. This makes AC #2 undeliverable by this ticket alone without an unacknowledged dependency.
findings.location: AC #2 / Implementation bullet 'close story' / ticket comment
scenarios: AC #2 cannot be tested because the gate enabling the close-profile check does not exist in this ticket's scope.
Reviewer expects close-path enforcement from this ticket but it is silently deferred to a sibling with no formal dependency link.

findings.finding: The plan's 'drift-refresh/default profile' language is mechanically ambiguous and, as written, would break progressive drift refresh: if compute_validity with the default profile is called inside drift_refresh_candidate, the existing code-drift check (stale-code verdict) fires on every drifted attestation — exactly the inputs drift_refresh_candidate is designed to process — causing it to always return None and disabling progressive refresh entirely.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Before returning a refresh candidate, drift_refresh_candidate calls compute_validity with the drift-refresh/default profile'
findings.suggested_fix: Define the drift-refresh profile as: check_code_freshness=false, check_regver=true, check_material=true, check_pins=true (when enabled). Update the plan to say 'drift_refresh_candidate calls compute_validity with the drift-refresh profile (code-drift check disabled)' and separately document that the default profile retains the code-drift check.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add tests/golden/config_surface.json to file_impact (update it with the new verify.enforce_plan_material_pins key to keep test_config_surface_golden.py green).
evidence: tests/golden/config_surface.json line 2: 'CI anti-regression guard for the config surface (story 36c7). Every key here must remain either LIVE (in rebar._config_schema._SECTIONS / a canonical env name) OR carry a tombstone/alias entry'
tests/unit/test_config_surface_golden.py exists and references the golden file
Existing verify.* keys (e.g., verify.require_plan_review_for_claim) appear in config_surface.json lines 41-44
verify.enforce_plan_material_pins is absent from config_surface.json
Plan file_impact does not include tests/golden/config_surface.json
findings.finding: tests/golden/config_surface.json is a CI anti-regression guard (test_config_surface_golden.py) that must be updated whenever a verify.* config key is added. The plan adds verify.enforce_plan_material_pins to _config_schema.py but does not name tests/golden/config_surface.json in file_impact, so the CI golden check will fail without this edit.
findings.location: file_impact list / Implementation bullet 'Add verify.enforce_plan_material_pins'
scenarios: test_config_surface_golden.py fails in CI because verify.enforce_plan_material_pins is added to _config_schema.py but not to the golden file.
make check fails even though the feature itself is correctly implemented.
findings.suggested_fix: Add {"path": "tests/golden/config_surface.json", "reason": "Register verify.enforce_plan_material_pins in the config surface golden file to satisfy the CI anti-regression guard."} to file_impact.

findings.finding: The plan says 'Before returning a refresh candidate, drift_refresh_candidate calls compute_validity with the drift-refresh/default profile; enabled non-current pin health returns None and forces a full review'. The actual drift_refresh_candidate (attest.py:146-179) does NOT call compute_validity at all — it performs its own inline checks (verify_signature, manifest_regver, manifest_material, manifest_deps, _rehash). This means the described pin-enforcement integration into drift_refresh_candidate is entirely new logic, not a migration of an existing compute_validity call.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Before returning a refresh candidate, drift_refresh_candidate calls compute_validity with the drift-refresh/default profile'

findings.finding: The plan names a 'close-profile consumer' and 'the close story' as existing consumers of `compute_validity` that need migrating, but no plan-review close gate exists in the codebase — the only plan-review validity check on the close path is the start-work gate (`plan_review_precheck`), not a close gate.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Migrate the existing plan-review callers claim_gate_check, idempotent reuse, drift_refresh_candidate, re-sign, audit/health, and the close story to this same function. The close profile sets check_code_freshness=false'; AC: 'Enabled pin drift invalidates the close-profile consumer when that gate is active.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (e.g., 'Unreadable or malformed enforce_plan_material_pins config fails open with a warning and does not change valid') to cover the explicitly-specified fail-open behavior.
evidence: Plan states: 'Keep optional unreadable configuration fail-open with a warning, matching existing gate posture; once enforcement is successfully enabled, validation fails closed.'
No AC covers: unreadable/unparseable/missing config key falls back to disabled mode with a warning observable to callers.
The 12 ACs cover well-formed true/false config states but never test the malformed-config code path.
findings.finding: The plan explicitly specifies that an unreadable `verify.enforce_plan_material_pins` configuration fails open with a warning, but no AC verifies this behavior — meaning a broken fail-open implementation (which silently enforces or silently disables) could pass all stated ACs.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria / Implementation: 'Keep optional unreadable configuration fail-open with a warning, matching existing gate posture'
scenarios: Config file is syntactically valid TOML/YAML but the key has an unexpected type (e.g., integer instead of boolean); enforcement silently activates or silently suppresses, causing live breakage undiscoverable until production.
Config file is unreadable at runtime; without a test, the fail-open path may accidentally fail-closed, breaking every attestation consumer.
findings.suggested_fix: Add: '- [ ] Unreadable or type-invalid verify.enforce_plan_material_pins configuration fails open (enforcement disabled) with a warning log, leaving valid unchanged for all consumers.' Pair with a focused config-parse test that injects a malformed config and asserts fail-open behavior.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that re-sign (resign_plan_review in resign.py) currently has NO call to compute_validity and that adding one is explicitly new work, not a migration of an existing call.
evidence: resign.py:86-111: staleness guard checks recorded_material vs current_material directly, never calls compute_validity.
resign.py:126: calls attest.sign_plan_review(verdict, ...) directly with no compute_validity gate.
compute_validity is not imported in resign.py.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 're-sign' as one of the consumers migrated to compute_validity with the profile parameter, but resign_plan_review (resign.py:35-148) currently performs its own staleness check (recorded_material vs current_material at line 101) and calls attest.sign_plan_review directly, without ever calling compute_validity.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Migrate plan-review callers claim_gate_check, idempotent reuse, drift_refresh_candidate, re-sign, audit/health, and the close story to this same function.'
scenarios: Implementer assumes re-sign already calls compute_validity and skips wiring in the pin-enforcement check, leaving AC5 ('Enabled pin drift prevents re-sign') unimplemented.
Pin drift on a prerequisite does not block resign_plan_review because the function never calls compute_validity to check pin health.

findings.finding: The plan lists 'the close story' as one of the consumers to 'Migrate … to this same function' (compute_validity with profile parameter). However, transition_close.py contains NO existing plan-review compute_validity call — it only calls compute_validity for completion-verifier via completion.py. There is no 'close-story plan-review consumer' to migrate; this is entirely new gate logic that must be added.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Migrate the existing plan-review callers claim_gate_check, idempotent reuse, drift_refresh_candidate, re-sign, audit/health, and the close story to this same function'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'compute_plan_review_validity' in the goal/preamble with 'compute_validity' to match the existing symbol and the plan's own clarification later in the Implementation section.
evidence: Plan text: 'Own the nested DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract returned as health beside compute_plan_review_validity existing top-level {valid, reason, verdict}'
Grep confirms only compute_validity exists at src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py:471
Plan later clarifies: 'Extend the existing attest.compute_validity(attestation, ticket_state, kind, *, repo_root=None, profile=PlanValidityProfile.DEFAULT) without renaming or wrapping it'
findings.finding: The plan's preamble names 'compute_plan_review_validity existing top-level {valid, reason, verdict}' as if this symbol already exists, but only compute_validity exists at attest.py:471. The plan's body later clarifies it means to extend compute_validity — but the goal/implementation opening line uses the non-existent name, which could cause an implementer to search for or create a wrong symbol.
findings.location: Implementation section, first sentence of the profile/profile extension bullet
scenarios: Implementer searches for compute_plan_review_validity, finds nothing, creates a duplicate wrapper instead of extending compute_validity in-place.
Code reviewer flags the symbol as missing, misunderstanding the intent.
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'returned as health beside compute_plan_review_validity existing top-level {valid, reason, verdict}' to 'returned as health beside the existing compute_validity top-level {valid, reason, verdict}'.

The concrete file and symbol for the 'audit' consumer are not identified, making this edit target ambiguous and potentially hallucinated.

A renderer or CLI command that surfaces health data is missed, leaving its output inconsistent with the new DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that wiring compute_validity into drift_refresh_candidate is NEW work this story introduces, not pre-existing behavior; the current function (attest.py:146-179) has no such call and must be modified.
evidence: attest.py:146-179: drift_refresh_candidate checks result.get('verified'), is_plan_review_manifest(manifest), manifest_regver vs registry_version, signed_material vs current_material_fingerprint, deps existence, and rehashes deps — NO call to compute_validity.
compute_validity is not imported or referenced within drift_refresh_candidate's code block.
The plan's Implementation section phrases this as the desired behavior but it reads ambiguously as a current-state assertion.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'Before returning a refresh candidate, drift_refresh_candidate calls compute_validity(kind=plan-review, profile=DEFAULT)' but reading attest.py:146-179 shows drift_refresh_candidate performs its own bespoke inline checks (registry version compare, signed_material compare, dep rehash) and does NOT call compute_validity at all.
findings.location: Implementation section, paragraph: 'Before returning a refresh candidate, drift_refresh_candidate calls compute_validity(kind=plan-review, profile=DEFAULT); if returned valid is false it returns None before the probe.'
scenarios: Implementer assumes drift_refresh_candidate already gates on compute_validity and skips adding the call, leaving pin-drift bypass open on the progressive refresh path.
AC 'Enabled pin drift prevents progressive drift refresh' fails because the new compute_validity pin check is never wired into drift_refresh_candidate.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `drift_refresh_candidate`'s internal freshness checks (regver, material, dep-presence) are REPLACED by the `compute_validity` pre-call or retained alongside it, and document the invariant explicitly.
evidence: Plan: 'Before returning a refresh candidate, drift_refresh_candidate calls compute_validity(kind=plan-review, profile=DEFAULT); if returned valid is false it returns None before the probe.'

attest.py drift_refresh_candidate (lines 165-178) performs its own regver, material, and dep-presence checks that are logically equivalent to parts of compute_validity's plan-review branch — these are not removed by the plan's stated migration.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that `drift_refresh_candidate` calls `compute_validity(kind=plan-review, profile=DEFAULT)` as a pre-filter, but `drift_refresh_candidate` currently performs its own parallel freshness checks (regver, material, deps at lines 165-179 of attest.py). The plan does not state whether the internal checks are removed (replaced by the `compute_validity` call) or retained alongside it. If retained, pin enforcement could produce contradictory outcomes: `compute_validity` returns `valid=false` on pin drift → `drift_refresh_candidate` returns `None` (correct), but the remaining internal checks then become dead code that silently diverges from the unified path if logic ever changes. If the internal checks are removed, that is a meaningful behavioral change the plan should acknowledge explicitly.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Before returning a refresh candidate, drift_refresh_candidate calls compute_validity(kind=plan-review, profile=DEFAULT); if returned valid is false it returns None before the probe.' / src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py lines 146-179
scenarios: Pin enforcement enabled + stale pin: compute_validity returns valid=false → drift_refresh_candidate returns None (correct, but internal checks are now dead).
Future developer edits internal checks unaware they are now redundant, creating a logic split with compute_validity.
findings.suggested_fix: State explicitly: 'The drift_refresh_candidate call to compute_validity replaces the internal regver/material/dep-presence checks; those internal checks are removed and validity (including pin status) flows exclusively from compute_validity.' This preserves the single-source-of-truth goal.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Co-locate a population rule for TargetPinDetail.current_fingerprint with the schema: specify null for stale-pin-missing (target not found) and malformed-pin (unparseable pin line), and the recomputed fingerprint string for current/stale-pin-drift cases.
evidence: 'current_fingerprint: string|null' — type declared but no population rule
'stale-pin-missing' — plan names this status for a deleted/missing pinned target but does not say current_fingerprint is null in this case
No protocol co-located with the schema describing the lookup logic: 'read current narrow material for canonical_id; if ticket not found → null; if read error → null; else → computed fingerprint'
findings.finding: TargetPinDetail.current_fingerprint is declared as 'string|null' but no processing protocol specifies WHEN it is null versus a populated string — particularly for stale-pin-missing (target deleted) and malformed-pin (parse error) cases. An implementer cannot determine from the spec alone whether current_fingerprint is null when the target ticket no longer exists, null when the fingerprint cannot be computed due to a read error, or a populated string for stale-pin-drift only.
findings.location: Implementation section, TargetPinDetail schema definition
scenarios: Renderer checks current_fingerprint == null to display 'target deleted' but implementation sets it to empty string '' for missing targets — UI shows no indicator
Audit consumer interprets null as malformed rather than missing, miscategorizes status

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify precisely how the compute_validity pre-call in drift_refresh_candidate relates to its existing inline checks: state whether (a) the inline checks are removed and compute_validity subsumes them, or (b) compute_validity is inserted as an additional gate before the inline checks, and if so which validity failures it is expected to see (should code-drift be excluded from the DEFAULT profile call here).
evidence: Plan: 'Before returning a refresh candidate, drift_refresh_candidate calls compute_validity(kind=plan-review, profile=DEFAULT)'
attest.py:165: 'if manifest_regver(manifest) != registry_version(repo_root): return None' — existing inline regver check
attest.py:169-172: existing inline material-fingerprint check
Plan does not say 'replace the existing inline checks' or 'keep the existing inline checks and add compute_validity as an additional gate'
findings.finding: The plan says 'Before returning a refresh candidate, drift_refresh_candidate calls compute_validity(kind=plan-review, profile=DEFAULT); if returned valid is false it returns None before the probe.' The existing drift_refresh_candidate (attest.py:146-179) already performs inline checks (regver, material fingerprint, deps) that would overlap with compute_validity. The plan does not specify whether these existing inline checks are removed, kept, or subsumed — creating ambiguous placement of validity logic that an implementer must resolve without guidance.
findings.location: Implementation section, drift_refresh_candidate protocol; src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py:146-179
scenarios: Implementer adds the compute_validity call but keeps all existing inline checks → double-checking some conditions; the deps drift check runs twice

Implementer removes the existing inline checks and delegates to compute_validity → compute_validity's code-freshness check (DEFAULT profile) now returns valid=false for legitimately drifted code, making drift_refresh_candidate always return None (defeating progressive refresh)

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for each AC that lacks one: (1) for 'no warning event persisted', name the assertion (e.g., `assert not any(e.kind == 'warning' for e in health.events)`); (2) for 'pin emission enabled', name the artifact or test that confirms emission still fires; (3) for 'malformed without raising', name the test fixture and the pytest invocation pattern.
evidence: 'Run focused attestation invalidation, reuse, drift-refresh, re-sign, and config tests' — no test file names, pytest -k patterns, or grep commands given
'No warning event is persisted for derived health' — no command described to assert absence of warning events in the event store or log
'verify.enforce_plan_material_pins=true leaves new pin emission enabled' — pin emission is not defined in the Implementation section and no step describes how to verify it
'without raising' in the malformed AC implies an exception-absence check, but no fixture or assertion command is described
findings.finding: The Verification section provides no concrete per-AC proving commands — only category descriptions ('Run focused attestation invalidation, reuse, drift-refresh, re-sign, and config tests'). Several ACs have no mapped proving step at all: 'No warning event is persisted for derived health', 'verify.enforce_plan_material_pins=true leaves new pin emission enabled', and 'Malformed manifest metadata returns malformed/invalid at every consumer without raising'. Without a concrete command anchored to each AC, an executor cannot produce verifiable evidence of completion.
findings.location: Verification section; ACs 'No warning event is persisted for derived health', 'verify.enforce_plan_material_pins=true/false leaves new pin emission enabled', 'Malformed manifest metadata returns malformed/invalid at every consumer without raising'
scenarios: Reviewer cannot confirm 'no warning event persisted' without a concrete store/log assertion command
'Pin emission enabled' is unverifiable if the executor doesn't know where emission is implemented or what artifact proves it is still active
Malformed-without-raising can be confirmed only by running a specific fixture with a try/except wrapper or pytest's raises-not-raised pattern — neither is described

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify exactly where `compute_validity` is called in `resign_plan_review`: e.g., 'Before calling `sign_plan_review`, `resign_plan_review` calls `compute_validity` on the current ticket state; if `valid=false` (including due to pin drift with enforcement enabled), it returns `ok=False` with the reason.'
evidence: Plan: 'Migrate plan-review callers … re-sign … to this same function.'
Plan: 'Enabled pin drift prevents re-sign.' (AC)
resign.py lines 51-135: reads sidecar, checks verdict=PASS and material fingerprint, then calls sign_plan_review directly — no compute_validity call exists in this path
ABSENCE: No specification of where in the resign flow compute_validity is invoked or what it checks
findings.finding: The `resign_plan_review` consumer migration to `compute_validity` is under-specified: the plan lists re-sign as a consumer to migrate, but `resign.py` currently reads only the sidecar's PASS verdict + material fingerprint and calls `sign_plan_review` directly — it never verifies the EXISTING attestation through `compute_validity`. With pin enforcement enabled, a re-sign that bypasses `compute_validity` would produce a fresh attestation on a plan whose related-material pins are stale/missing, defeating enforcement on the re-sign path. The plan should specify WHAT the re-sign consumer must check via `compute_validity` (specifically: does it check the pre-existing attestation, or does it ensure pin validity before minting the new one, and at what point in the re-sign flow).
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Migrate plan-review callers claim_gate_check, idempotent reuse, drift_refresh_candidate, re-sign, audit/health, and the close story to this same function.' / src/rebar/llm/plan_review/resign.py
scenarios: Enforcement enabled, a child ticket was edited (pin drift). `resign_plan_review` is called: sidecar has PASS + matching material fingerprint. It calls sign_plan_review without pin check → new attestation minted with stale pins → claim gate passes (pins already validated in old attestation's manifest, not recomputed).
AC 'Enabled pin drift prevents re-sign' fails because the re-sign path never triggers compute_validity.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the implementation: 'resign_plan_review calls compute_validity(existing_attestation, ticket_state, kind=plan-review, profile=DEFAULT) after the material-fingerprint check; if valid=false it returns ok=False without signing, reporting the validity reason.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify what happens when verify.enforce_plan_material_pins config becomes unreadable *after* having been successfully read as true — specify whether the session-cached value is used (fail-closed) or fail-open applies, and document this as the rollback/degraded-mode contract.
evidence: 'Keep optional unreadable configuration fail-open with a warning, matching existing gate posture; once enforcement is successfully enabled, validation fails closed.'

These two clauses are in direct tension: fail-open on unread config vs. fail-closed once enabled — the plan does not resolve which wins when config becomes unreadable after being successfully read as true.
findings.finding: The plan states 'Keep optional unreadable configuration fail-open with a warning' AND 'once enforcement is successfully enabled, validation fails closed.' If the config file becomes unreadable after enforcement was successfully enabled (e.g., permission change, corruption), the fail-open behavior would silently disable enforcement mid-operation — a security regression with no explicit resolution specified.
findings.location: Implementation section, final bullet: 'Keep optional unreadable configuration fail-open with a warning, matching existing gate posture; once enforcement is successfully enabled, validation fails closed.'
scenarios: Config file permissions are changed mid-deployment; enforcement silently reverts to advisory without operator awareness.
Config is temporarily unreadable during a rolling restart; attestation gates open unexpectedly.
findings.suggested_fix: Cache the successfully-read enforcement value for the duration of a single compute_validity call and document that transient unreadability after a successful read does not revert enforcement. Reserve fail-open only for the case where the key has never been successfully read (i.e., absent or unreadable at first access).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the plan's naming: replace 'compute_plan_review_validity existing top-level' with 'compute_validity (attest.py:471)' throughout, making clear the plan extends the existing function rather than naming a new one.
evidence: Grep for 'compute_plan_review_validity' returns zero source-file hits; only cache/review artifacts mention it.
attest.py:471 defines `def compute_validity(attestation, ticket_state, kind, *, repo_root=None)` — not compute_plan_review_validity.

The plan later contradicts itself: 'Extend the existing attest.compute_validity(attestation, ticket_state, kind, *, repo_root=None, profile=PlanValidityProfile.DEFAULT)' — confirming the target is compute_validity, not a function named compute_plan_review_validity.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'compute_plan_review_validity existing top-level {valid, reason, verdict}' as if a function by that name exists; grep and file read confirm the only matching function is `compute_validity` at attest.py:471. No symbol named `compute_plan_review_validity` exists anywhere in the codebase.
findings.location: Implementation section, paragraph: 'Own the nested DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract returned as health beside compute_plan_review_validity existing top-level {valid, reason, verdict}'
scenarios: Implementer searches for compute_plan_review_validity to find the extension point and finds nothing, causing confusion about which function to extend.
A reader infers the plan describes an already-existing boundary that pre-dates this work, leading to incorrect scope assumptions.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'beside compute_plan_review_validity existing top-level {valid, reason, verdict}' with 'beside the existing compute_validity top-level {valid, reason, verdict}' to remove the false premise.

findings.finding: The plan specifies that `current_fingerprint` in `TargetPinDetail` is `string|null` but never specifies the fallback decision rule when a pinned target's fingerprint CANNOT BE READ at health-compute time (e.g., due to a read error, not because the target is deleted). The plan states 'archived targets remain readable' and the config fail-open rule, but neither covers a transient read failure on a live related target.
findings.location: Implementation section — TargetPinDetail schema and 'When enabled, drift ... invalidate' paragraph

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the read lifecycle for verify.enforce_plan_material_pins: whether it is read once per compute_validity call, once per process start, or once per request-context — and confirm the value is consistent across all six consumers within a single attestation operation.
evidence: Six consumers enumerated but no specification of config read lifecycle (once at startup vs. per-call vs. per-request)
ABSENCE: no 'config is loaded at startup' or 'config is read per call' language anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The plan enumerates six consumers of verify.enforce_plan_material_pins (claim_gate_check, idempotent reuse, drift_refresh_candidate, re-sign, audit/health, close) but does not specify whether the config value is read fresh per compute_validity invocation or loaded once at process/module startup. This leaves the read-freshness lifecycle undefined, creating ambiguity about consistency within a single attestation flow and the effect of live config changes.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Migrate plan-review callers claim_gate_check, idempotent reuse, drift_refresh_candidate, re-sign, audit/health, and the close story to this same function.'
scenarios: Operator flips enforcement on; in-flight compute_validity calls running against a cached=false value bypass enforcement.
Config is read six times across a single attestation lifecycle, returning different values if a concurrent write races mid-flow.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a precise aggregation algorithm co-located with the DerivedPlanReviewHealth schema: e.g., 'top-level pin_status = max severity across targets by the fixed ordering, or legacy-unpinned if targets is empty AND no pin lines exist in the manifest.'
evidence: 'Aggregate target status by fixed severity malformed-pin > stale-pin-missing > stale-pin-drift > current' — states the ordering but not the aggregation algorithm
No co-located pseudocode or rule for: take max(severity(t.pin_status) for t in targets) and map back to the aggregate pin_status string
The legacy-unpinned condition ('occurs only for a wholly unpinned manifest with an empty targets list') is a special-case that creates an additional branch not covered by the ordering alone
findings.finding: The aggregation rule that reduces the per-target list into the single top-level DerivedPlanReviewHealth.pin_status is not co-located with the TargetPinDetail schema. The plan names a severity ordering (malformed-pin > stale-pin-missing > stale-pin-drift > current) but never specifies the algorithm: when targets have mixed statuses (e.g., some current + some stale-pin-drift), is the aggregate the maximum-severity status? What is the aggregate when targets is empty but the manifest has pins? The decision rule an implementer needs to write the aggregation is absent from the schema definition.
findings.location: Implementation section, DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract definition
scenarios: Implementer writes a reduce that uses first-match instead of max-severity, silently producing wrong aggregate for mixed-status target lists
Renderers consuming the top-level pin_status produce inconsistent displays when targets mixes stale-pin-drift and malformed-pin entries
findings.suggested_fix: Under the DerivedPlanReviewHealth definition, add: 'Aggregation rule: pin_status = the highest-severity value from {t.pin_status for t in targets} per the ordering malformed-pin > stale-pin-missing > stale-pin-drift > current; if targets is empty AND the manifest contains no plan-material-pin lines, pin_status = legacy-unpinned; if targets is empty AND the manifest does contain pin lines (all malformed), pin_status = malformed-pin.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback or back-out strategy for the caller migration step — e.g., an expand-contract sequence (introduce compute_validity alongside existing implementations, migrate callers one at a time, remove old paths after validation) or a documented revert procedure.
evidence: Implementation: 'Migrate plan-review callers claim_gate_check, idempotent reuse, drift_refresh_candidate, re-sign, audit/health, and the close story to this same function.'

ABSENCE: No section describes a phased migration, a compatibility shim to keep old call-sites working while new ones are validated, or a revert procedure if a caller regression is discovered post-migration.
findings.finding: The plan migrates multiple existing callers (claim_gate_check, idempotent reuse, drift_refresh_candidate, re-sign, audit/health, close story) to a unified `compute_validity` function but provides no explicit rollback or back-out plan if this migration introduces a regression. The Implementation describes the target state but not an expand-contract sequence or a reversibility mechanism for the migration step.
findings.location: Implementation — 'Migrate plan-review callers claim_gate_check, idempotent reuse, drift_refresh_candidate, re-sign, audit/health, and the close story to this same function.'
scenarios: A regression introduced in one migrated caller (e.g., drift_refresh_candidate) is discovered in production; there is no documented path to revert that caller to its prior implementation without reverting the entire change.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or AGENTS.md section capturing the DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract invariants, PlanValidityProfile semantics, and the rationale for fixed severity ordering so future consumers and child 5967 have a stable reference.
evidence: 'Own the nested DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract returned as health beside compute_plan_review_validity existing top-level {valid, reason, verdict}'
'Define PlanValidityProfile with exactly DEFAULT and CLOSE'
'Migrate plan-review callers claim_gate_check, idempotent reuse, drift_refresh_candidate, re-sign, audit/health, and the close story to this same function'
ABSENCE: no mention of ADR, AGENTS.md, or design-doc update in the plan
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural contract (DerivedPlanReviewHealth with a fixed severity ordering, TargetPinDetail schema, and PlanValidityProfile DEFAULT/CLOSE modes) and a new cross-component pattern affecting six named consumers, but no ADR, AGENTS.md entry, or design-doc update is called out.
findings.location: Implementation section — DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract and PlanValidityProfile definition; Verification section
scenarios: A future engineer adding a new consumer needs to understand why DEFAULT vs CLOSE exist, why severity ordering is fixed, and why legacy-unpinned is always valid — none of which are discoverable without reading this ticket.
Child 5967 extends the same contract; without a captured design decision, the extension point and evolution rules are implicit.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief 'Design decisions' subsection to the plan (or a referenced ADR file path) covering: (1) why severity ordering is fixed rather than configurable, (2) why legacy-unpinned is always valid in both modes, (3) why CLOSE ignores code freshness but still applies pin validity, and (4) the extension contract for child 5967.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify at least one push-based observability path (e.g., a structured log line at DEBUG/INFO level per advisory drift observation) so operators can detect drift accumulation without polling each attestation, while keeping the AC constraint that no warning *event* is persisted.
evidence: 'When disabled, report the same observations as advisory while valid remains true'
'No warning event is persisted for derived health' (AC)
ABSENCE: no mention of logging, metrics, or trace attributes for advisory drift observations
findings.finding: When enforcement is disabled, advisory pin-drift observations are returned only in the DerivedPlanReviewHealth struct (a pull-based return value). The plan explicitly prohibits persisting warning events for derived health (AC: 'No warning event is persisted for derived health'), leaving no push-based observability path (log line, metric counter, or trace attribute) for operators to detect silent drift accumulation before they choose to enable enforcement.
findings.location: Implementation — 'When disabled, report the same observations as advisory while valid remains true'; AC — 'No warning event is persisted for derived health'
scenarios: An operator enables enforcement after a period of advisory-only mode and finds many attestations are now invalid — they had no signal that drift was accumulating.
A monitoring dashboard has no counter to alert on growing pin-drift rate in a repository.
findings.suggested_fix: Emit a single structured log line (e.g., `logger.info('pin_drift_advisory', canonical_id=..., pin_status=..., attestation_id=...)`) per advisory drift observation inside compute_validity when enforcement is disabled; this satisfies 'no warning event persisted' (no DB write) while giving operators a searchable signal.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm PlanValidityProfile enum is justified: either identify ≥3 existing call-sites that need distinct profile behavior today, or collapse the CLOSE distinction into a `check_code_freshness: bool = True` keyword argument on `compute_validity` to avoid a premature 2-value enum abstraction.
evidence: Plan text: 'For kind=completion-verifier the profile is ignored and existing completion.py callers never parse pins. Migrate plan-review callers claim_gate_check, idempotent reuse, drift_refresh_candidate, re-sign, audit/health, and the close story to this same function. DEFAULT runs every existing check including code freshness; CLOSE runs the same checks except code freshness.'
Only 1 of 6 listed consumers (the close story) uses CLOSE; the other 5 use DEFAULT — the enum adds generality for 1 current use-case.
Child 5967 is cited: 'child 5967 later adds phase compatibility to both' — but a sibling story's future deliverable does not satisfy Rule-of-Three for this plan.
Existing compute_validity at attest.py:471 already serves all 6 consumers without a profile parameter; the behavioral difference (skip code freshness) could be a single boolean kwarg.
findings.finding: PlanValidityProfile (DEFAULT/CLOSE) is a 2-value enum where only one value (CLOSE) produces distinct behavior, and only the single 'close story' consumer uses it — the other 5 named consumers (claim_gate_check, idempotent reuse, drift_refresh_candidate, re-sign, audit/health) all use DEFAULT. Rule-of-Three is not met for a distinct enum abstraction: the behavioral divergence is 1 consumer (CLOSE) vs 5 (DEFAULT). Child 5967 is cited as future extension but cannot satisfy the current story's ROT requirement.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Define PlanValidityProfile with exactly DEFAULT and CLOSE. Extend the existing attest.compute_validity(…, *, …, profile=PlanValidityProfile.DEFAULT)'
scenarios: An implementer adds enum boilerplate, a profile dispatch branch, and updates all call-sites — for a single behavioral variation (skip_code_freshness) that could be expressed as a keyword argument with no new type.
If child 5967 is descoped or restructured, PlanValidityProfile.CLOSE remains a singleton enum value serving one call-site permanently.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `PlanValidityProfile` with a `check_code_freshness: bool = True` keyword argument on `compute_validity`. The close consumer passes `check_code_freshness=False`; all other consumers use the default. No enum import, no migration, identical behavior. Revisit the enum if child 5967 adds a third genuinely distinct profile.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add docs/plan-review-gate.md to file_impact to document the new profile parameter and DerivedPlanReviewHealth return shape.
evidence: docs/plan-review-gate.md:379: 'plan_review.attest.compute_validity(attestation, ticket_state, kind) dispatcher'
docs/plan-review-gate.md:383: 'Invariant: gates call compute_validity on a certified attestation'
Plan extends compute_validity with a new profile parameter and DerivedPlanReviewHealth return value
docs/plan-review-gate.md is not listed in file_impact
findings.finding: docs/plan-review-gate.md documents compute_validity as the canonical gate boundary and is referenced in ADR 0009 and by downstream consumers, but it is not named in file_impact. Adding a profile parameter to compute_validity's signature and returning a DerivedPlanReviewHealth alongside the existing dict will make the documentation stale.
findings.location: file_impact list; docs/plan-review-gate.md lines 379-425
scenarios: Docs show the old 3-arg compute_validity signature after this ticket ships, misleading future implementers.
ADR 0009 cross-reference to compute_validity becomes stale.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile DerivedPlanReviewHealth ownership with the parent: either (a) include phase_status as a stub/placeholder in the contract this story defines (with a sentinel value until 5967 populates it), or (b) update the parent epic to explicitly authorize the split-ownership model and document the interim schema.
evidence: Parent (jira-reb-1160) Enforcement and rollout: 'compute_plan_review_validity preserves its top-level {valid, reason, verdict} contract and adds health: DerivedPlanReviewHealth. That nested contract has pin_status exactly current, stale-pin-drift, stale-pin-missing, malformed-pin, or legacy-unpinned; phase_status exactly compatible, incompatible, or malformed; enforced boolean; and targets: list[TargetPinDetail].'
Leaf plan: 'It has pin_status exactly current, stale-pin-drift, stale-pin-missing, malformed-pin, or legacy-unpinned; enforced boolean; and targets: list[TargetPinDetail].' — phase_status is absent.

Leaf plan: 'Child 5967 extends the same contract with phase_status and phase compatibility after it introduces the phase manifest format.' — re-assigns phase_status ownership to a sibling story, which the parent does not authorize.
findings.finding: The leaf omits phase_status from DerivedPlanReviewHealth and re-assigns its ownership to sibling 5967 as a future 'extension,' but the parent epic defines DerivedPlanReviewHealth as a single unified contract that already includes phase_status (exactly compatible|incompatible|malformed) alongside pin_status, enforced, and targets. The parent does not authorize a split-ownership model where this story delivers an incomplete contract and 5967 extends it.
findings.location: Implementation section, paragraph 2: 'Own the nested DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract ... Child 5967 extends the same contract with phase_status and phase compatibility'
scenarios: If 5967 is delayed or descoped, DerivedPlanReviewHealth will be missing the phase_status field the parent guarantees is part of the contract, breaking consumers that expect the full schema.
Code review or CI may accept a partial DerivedPlanReviewHealth struct from this story without noticing the missing field, deferring integration pain to 5967's merge.
findings.suggested_fix: Add phase_status to the DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract defined in this story as a stub field (e.g., phase_status: str | None = None or an explicit 'pending' sentinel) that 5967 fills in with the real compatible/incompatible/malformed values. This keeps the parent-defined schema shape intact from day one while preserving the delivery ordering.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'new pin emission' in the Implementation section (name the config key or code path that controls it) and state explicitly that `verify.enforce_plan_material_pins` must not affect it, so the AC is self-contained.
evidence: The Implementation section defines only `verify.enforce_plan_material_pins` and its effect on validation; it does not mention a separate pin-emission subsystem or flag
'new pin emission enabled' — 'new' and 'emission' have no referent in the plan body
An executor must infer or investigate where pin emission is controlled — a real design-knowledge gap, not an obvious convention
findings.finding: The AC 'verify.enforce_plan_material_pins=true leaves new pin emission enabled' refers to 'new pin emission' which is not defined or described anywhere in the Implementation section of this plan. An executor cannot determine what system, function, or configuration governs pin emission, whether it is a separate flag, or how to verify it was not accidentally disabled by adding the enforcement flag. This is an implicit scope boundary on a subsystem outside the plan.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'verify.enforce_plan_material_pins=true leaves new pin emission enabled' and 'verify.enforce_plan_material_pins=false leaves new pin emission enabled'
scenarios: Executor may misconfigure the enforcement flag to inadvertently disable emission and have no AC-specified way to detect it
Without knowing what 'pin emission' means, the executor cannot write the required test
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Implementation section: 'Pin emission (the writing of signed pins during attestation creation) is governed by [config key / function name] and is orthogonal to verify.enforce_plan_material_pins; setting either value of enforce_plan_material_pins must leave that emission path unchanged.' Then the AC becomes verifiable by checking that emission config key is unmodified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a sentence to the Implementation section explicitly stating that the `verify.enforce_plan_material_pins` flag controls only validity enforcement and has no effect on pin emission, and describe what 'pin emission' means in this context (e.g., which step generates/writes signed pins) so the two emission ACs are verifiable.
evidence: AC: 'verify.enforce_plan_material_pins=true leaves new pin emission enabled.'
AC: 'verify.enforce_plan_material_pins=false leaves new pin emission enabled.'

ABSENCE: The Implementation section describes only enforcement/invalidation behavior for the flag; no sentence addresses pin emission, its continued enablement, or how emission is decoupled from enforcement.
findings.finding: Two ACs assert that `verify.enforce_plan_material_pins=true` and `=false` each leave 'new pin emission enabled', but the Implementation body contains no description of pin emission behavior, its relationship to the enforcement flag, or any mechanism that would preserve emission independently of enforcement. These ACs have no corresponding described work.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'verify.enforce_plan_material_pins=true leaves new pin emission enabled' and 'verify.enforce_plan_material_pins=false leaves new pin emission enabled'
scenarios: An implementer reads the Implementation section, finds no guidance on emission, and either conflates enforcement with emission suppression or skips the emission-preservation requirement entirely, leaving the ACs unverifiable.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Implementation section, under the `verify.enforce_plan_material_pins` description, add: 'The flag exclusively controls enforcement of existing pins; it does not suppress or alter pin generation. Pin emission — the signing and writing of new fingerprint pins — proceeds identically regardless of this setting.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete motivation section (or Verification pre-condition) that shows the current behaviour: name at least one consumer that currently passes a stale-pin attestation when it should not (or currently raises where it should return advisory), with an Expected-vs-Actual or a pointer to a failing test, so the agent has a baseline to validate the change against.
evidence: Goal: 'Use signed pins as the single related-material validity check across every attestation lifecycle consumer' — asserts a desired end-state but does not show the current state is broken.
'all renderers consume this contract rather than duplicating strings' — implies current duplication but provides no reproduction showing that duplication causes incorrect outcomes.

'Migrate plan-review callers claim_gate_check, idempotent reuse, drift_refresh_candidate, re-sign, audit/health, and the close story to this same function' — implies these callers currently use separate paths, but no example of drift passing when it should fail (or vice versa) is given.
No Verification section includes a 'current behaviour / repro' step — only 'Run focused … tests' and 'Run make check'.
findings.finding: The plan proposes adding DerivedPlanReviewHealth, verify.enforce_plan_material_pins, PlanValidityProfile, and migrating multiple callers to a centralised compute_validity, but never demonstrates that the current behaviour is wrong or insufficient — there is no reproduction (no Expected/Actual, no failing test scenario), no named defect, and no stated user or system problem that the current per-consumer, non-pin-enforcing validity paths cause. The motivation is asserted ('single validity check, no string duplication') but not grounded in observed current behaviour.
findings.location: ## Goal / ## Implementation — entire plan
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Current behaviour' or 'Motivation' sub-section under ## Goal that reproduces one failing scenario, e.g.: 'Currently, drift_refresh_candidate does not call compute_validity with pin checks; a stale-pin attestation therefore returns a non-None refresh candidate even when enforce_plan_material_pins=true is set, causing [observable outcome]. This plan closes that gap.' This gives the agent a falsifiable baseline.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'duplicate pin' in the implementation section (e.g., same canonical_id appearing more than once in a manifest) and specify its deterministic outcome (treated as malformed-pin, or first-entry wins), so the AC is testable against a clear oracle.
evidence: Implementation section defines pin_status values as: current, stale-pin-drift, stale-pin-missing, malformed-pin, legacy-unpinned — none of which map to a 'duplicate' state.
TargetPinDetail schema has no field for duplicate detection.
No implementation prose describes how a duplicate pin (same canonical_id appearing twice?) is detected or resolved.
findings.finding: The AC 'Missing/deleted, archived, malformed, duplicate, and re-enable scenarios are deterministic' lists 'duplicate' as a scenario, but the implementation section does not define what a duplicate pin is, how it arises, or what the deterministic outcome should be — making this AC untestable as written.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: 'Missing/deleted, archived, malformed, duplicate, and re-enable scenarios are deterministic'
scenarios: A manifest contains two entries with the same canonical_id; without a specified behavior, the test author must invent the oracle, risking a tautological/change-detector test.
Implementations may diverge (first-wins vs. last-wins vs. malformed) with no spec to arbitrate.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and document which read path makes archived targets' material fingerprints available for pin health computation, and ensure it explicitly sets exclude_archived=False (or equivalent) where needed.
evidence: src/rebar/graph/_graph.py:38-39: 'exclude_archived: When True (default), archived tickets are excluded from children and blockers lists. Pass False to include archived tickets.'
No existing function in src/rebar/llm/plan_review/ is identified that bypasses exclude_archived for fingerprint computation.
current_material_fingerprint (attest.py:769-800) calls _reads.list_tickets(parent=...) with no exclude_archived=False override.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'Archived targets remain readable' as a behavioral guarantee for pin health computation, but the codebase's graph module explicitly excludes archived tickets by default (src/rebar/graph/_graph.py:38-39: 'exclude_archived: When True (default), archived tickets are excluded from children and blockers lists'). No existing code path is identified that can fetch material fingerprints for archived targets.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'When enabled, drift, malformed pins, or a deleted/missing pinned target invalidate the attestation. Archived targets remain readable.'
scenarios: A pinned prerequisite ticket is archived; the plan says it 'remains readable' but current_material_fingerprint cannot reach it through the default graph reads, causing unexpected stale-pin-missing or unverifiable result.
The guarantee 'archived targets remain readable' rests on an unverified mechanism that may require explicit plumbing (exclude_archived=False) not mentioned in the plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly define 'wholly unpinned' as 'the manifest carries zero `pin:` lines for related targets (children/prerequisites)' — distinct from the existing `dep:` code-drift lines — so the `legacy-unpinned` parser boundary is unambiguous.
evidence: Plan: 'legacy-unpinned occurs only for a wholly unpinned manifest with an empty targets list'
manifest.py build_manifest already emits 'dep <hash> <path>' lines for all existing attestations — these are code-drift deps, not related-material pins
ABSENCE: The plan never distinguishes 'no pin: lines for targets' from 'no dep: lines'
findings.finding: The plan defines `legacy-unpinned` as 'a wholly unpinned manifest with an empty targets list' but does not specify the parsing boundary between existing `dep <hash> <path>` lines (code-drift dependencies — already present in all manifests) and the new `pin:` lines for related-material targets. A pre-existing attestation has `dep:` lines but zero `pin:` lines. Without explicitly stating that 'wholly unpinned' means zero `pin:` lines for related targets (not zero `dep:` lines), implementers may incorrectly classify pre-existing attestations as non-legacy (e.g., by checking for any dep content rather than specifically pin content).
findings.location: Implementation section: 'TargetPinDetail is exactly {…}; legacy-unpinned has an empty targets list. Compare signed pins against current narrow material to populate it.' / DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract definition
scenarios: A pre-enforcement attestation has `dep:` lines for file-impact paths but no `pin:` lines. Implementer checks `bool(manifest_deps(manifest))` to determine 'wholly unpinned' and incorrectly treats it as non-legacy.
With enforcement enabled, a pre-existing attestation is misclassified as `stale-pin-missing` instead of `legacy-unpinned`, making valid=false and breaking the safe-rollout invariant.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract: 'A manifest is wholly-unpinned iff it contains no lines beginning with the `pin:` prefix (the new related-material pin marker); the existing `dep <hash> <path>` code-drift lines do not affect this determination.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive rationale for why pin health is embedded in `compute_validity`'s return value rather than exposed as a separate `compute_pin_health` function (e.g., 'co-locating ensures health and validity always agree on enforcement state; separate calls risk TOCTOU inconsistency between health and validity reads').
evidence: Plan: 'Extend the existing attest.compute_validity(…) without renaming or wrapping it.'
Plan: 'Own the nested DerivedPlanReviewHealth contract returned as health beside compute_plan_review_validity existing top-level {valid, reason, verdict}'
ABSENCE: No stated reason for bundling health into compute_validity vs. a dedicated health query
findings.finding: The plan does not state a positive rationale for extending the existing `compute_validity` function signature (adding `profile=PlanValidityProfile.DEFAULT`) rather than the structurally different alternative of a standalone `compute_pin_health(attestation, *, repo_root) -> DerivedPlanReviewHealth` orthogonal to `compute_validity`. The chosen approach bundles pin-health reporting into the validity-check call, coupling the health contract's evolution to `compute_validity`'s interface. The alternative would keep validity a pure boolean-reason pair and health a separately-queryable struct, making it easier for consumers that only want health (e.g., audit/health endpoint) to avoid triggering full validity logic. The plan gives no rationale for why coupling these is preferable.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Define PlanValidityProfile with exactly DEFAULT and CLOSE. Extend the existing attest.compute_validity(attestation, ticket_state, kind, *, repo_root=None, profile=PlanValidityProfile.DEFAULT) without renaming or wrapping it.'
scenarios: A consumer (audit/health endpoint) wants pin health without validity side-effects — it must call compute_validity and ignore validity fields, carrying the full validity overhead.
Adding phase_status to health (child 5967) requires further extending compute_validity's return shape, increasing coupling.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a manifest pin-line wire format spec analogous to the existing `dep <sha256> <path>` encoding — define the prefix token, field order, and separator for canonical_id, role, and pinned_fingerprint so manifest_pins() has a single unambiguous encoding to parse and emit.
evidence: Plan states 'Compare signed pins against current narrow material' and names manifest_pins() but provides no manifest line format spec
Existing dep lines are specified: 'dep <sha256> <path>' in manifest.py _DEP_PREFIX/'dep'
TargetPinDetail struct fields (canonical_id, role, pinned_fingerprint, current_fingerprint, pin_status) are defined in-memory but not serialized to/from manifest lines
Child 5967 AC: 'The completion-verifier branch never invokes manifest_pins()' — implies manifest_pins() is a stable, parseable API, but no encoding is given for what it parses
findings.finding: The plan names manifest_pins() as a new manifest parser and references pin lines in the signed manifest (e.g., 'Compare signed pins against current narrow material'), but never specifies the wire format of those pin lines. The existing dep-hash encoding (dep <sha256> <path>) is fully specified, but the analogous pin encoding — what a pin line looks like in the manifest list, how canonical_id, role (child|prerequisite), and pinned_fingerprint are serialized — is absent. This leaves the novel line format as an unanchored design choice that implementers and child 5967 consumers must rediscover or invent independently.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Compare signed pins against current narrow material to populate it; all renderers consume this contract rather than duplicating strings' and AC 'The completion-verifier branch never invokes manifest_pins()'
scenarios: Implementer chooses an ad-hoc encoding (e.g., 'pin <role> <fingerprint> <canonical_id>') that child 5967 or an audit renderer cannot round-trip correctly
A malformed-pin scenario (AC: 'Malformed manifest metadata returns malformed/invalid') cannot be reliably tested without a canonical encoding spec
Re-sign path reconstructs pins from sidecar — if the encoding is underdetermined, refreshed attestations may differ from original
findings.suggested_fix: Define a `_PIN_PREFIX = 'pin'` constant and a line format `pin <role> <pinned_fingerprint> <canonical_id>` (fixed-width fingerprint before the id, matching dep's `dep <sha256> <path>` ordering), and document it alongside the TargetPinDetail contract in the Implementation section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a post-rollback schema-state assertion for both persisted format changes: specify (a) the exact behavior of pre-feature `compute_validity`/`drift_refresh_candidate`/`claim_gate_check` when they encounter a manifest containing `plan-material-pin:` lines (error, safe-skip, or wrong-result), and (b) whether an older reducer re-compacting a phase-bearing SNAPSHOT preserves or strips `plan_review_phase` — and document a compensating-forward path if the latter is destructive.
evidence: Plan says 'legacy-unpinned occurs only for a wholly unpinned manifest' — forward compat for new readers of old manifests is addressed, but the reverse (old readers of new manifests) is not asserted at the state level.
Plan says 'Carry it through SNAPSHOT compaction' (for plan_review_phase in child 5967), but never asserts what an older reducer binary does on re-compaction of a phase-bearing snapshot.
T11 spec: 'the plan must assert on the schema STATE AFTER rollback, not merely that rollback exits 0 — a rollback can exit 0 while leaving the schema in an incorrect intermediate state.'

Existing `_version.py` forward-compat mechanism (preserve-and-ignore for unknown KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES) governs event-type dispatch, NOT manifest line content — the manifest parser in `manifest.py` has no equivalent ignore-unknown-line contract for old binaries.

ABSENCE: no plan section states 'a pre-feature `compute_validity` reading a post-feature manifest will produce result X' or 'a pre-feature reducer re-compacting a phase-bearing SNAPSHOT will preserve/strip plan_review_phase'.
findings.finding: The plan introduces two persisted-format changes — `plan-material-pin:` lines embedded in immutable signed SIGNATURE-event manifests, and `plan_review_phase` in reducer SNAPSHOT compiled-state — but never asserts the schema STATE AFTER a code rollback. The plan acknowledges forward-compat ('unknown pin lines ignored by older verifiers'), but does not state what pre-feature code does when it reads a pin-bearing manifest at validity/claim time (does `compute_validity`, `drift_refresh_candidate`, or `claim_gate_check` produce a wrong result, raise, or safely skip?). Likewise for a phase-bearing SNAPSHOT: if the older reducer re-compacts it, does `plan_review_phase` survive or get silently stripped, and is the resulting compiled-state correct? Per T11, a rollback-exits-0 assertion is insufficient; the post-rollback schema STATE must be specified.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'plan-material-pin:' manifest lines; child 5967 SNAPSHOT compaction of plan_review_phase
scenarios: Code is rolled back after pin-bearing SIGNATURE events are written; old `drift_refresh_candidate` or `claim_gate_check` parses the manifest — if any old parser errors on an unrecognised line prefix, the claim gate could break for all already-pinned tickets.

Code is rolled back after phase-bearing SNAPSHOTs are written; old reducer re-compacts a ticket — if it silently drops `plan_review_phase`, the phase field is lost permanently on the next compaction, corrupting the projection.
Partial rollout where some replicas run new code (writing pins) and others run old code (reading manifests), creating a heterogeneous state with undefined validity behavior.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Rollback state' sub-section to Implementation: (a) for manifest lines — confirm that old parsers use prefix-match ignore-unknown-line semantics (cite the existing `manifest_deps`/`manifest_material` pattern); if they do not, add an explicit ignore-unrecognised-line contract to old parsers before shipping; (b) for SNAPSHOT — assert that the older reducer's `process_snapshot` passes unknown keys through unchanged (analogous to the event-type preserve-and-ignore contract in `_version.py`), or acknowledge that plan_review_phase must be re-bootstrapped from ticket status on the next post-rollback reduction and describe the resulting state.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a co-located processing rule for PlanValidityProfile.CLOSE: enumerate exactly which validity checks (scoped deps rehash, whole-HEAD sha comparison, or both) are suppressed, and which are retained (reopen, regver, material, pin).
evidence: 'CLOSE runs the same checks except code freshness' — behavioral statement without naming the internal branching mechanism
Existing compute_validity plan-review branch at attest.py:567-599 has no 'code freshness' flag — it falls straight into deps/head-SHA checks
No co-located spec of which lines/blocks inside compute_validity are conditioned on profile==CLOSE
findings.finding: PlanValidityProfile.CLOSE is defined with the behavioral difference 'runs the same checks except code freshness', but the processing protocol — which specific internal check or parameter is suppressed — is not specified co-located with the PlanValidityProfile enum definition. The existing compute_validity plan-review branch (attest.py:543-625) performs code-drift checks via deps re-hashing (line 571) and whole-HEAD freshness (line 588); neither a 'check_code_freshness' parameter nor a branch point is named in the plan that an implementer can key on.
findings.location: Implementation section, PlanValidityProfile definition and compute_validity extension
scenarios: Implementer skips both deps re-hash AND regver check for CLOSE (over-broad suppression), allowing stale-regver attestations through the close gate
Implementer skips only the whole-HEAD fallback but not the scoped deps rehash for CLOSE, inconsistently applying the profile

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the Implementation: specify the exception type (ManifestFormatError or equivalent) raised by manifest_pins() on malformed input, the catch location (inside compute_validity's pin-evaluation block), and the mapping rule to pin_status=malformed-pin / health.enforced-valid=false.
evidence: AC 14: 'Malformed manifest metadata returns malformed/invalid at every consumer without raising'
Implementation body has no sub-step describing: 'catch ManifestFormatError from manifest_pins(); populate health.pin_status=malformed-pin; set valid=false if enforced'
The plan mentions ManifestFormatError in cached sibling-plan context but does not define the exception type or the catch boundary in this plan
findings.finding: The plan states 'Malformed manifest metadata returns malformed/invalid at every consumer without raising' (AC 14) but the Implementation body provides no fallback protocol specifying which layer catches a parse error from manifest_pins() and how it maps to pin_status=malformed-pin without propagating the exception. The plan instructs an executor to handle this gracefully but does not co-locate the try/except boundary or the mapping rule with the schema or the compute_validity extension.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria: 'Malformed manifest metadata returns malformed/invalid at every consumer without raising'; Implementation section (no corresponding sub-step)
scenarios: Implementer catches Exception broadly in compute_validity's pin-check block but sets verdict='error' instead of 'malformed-pin', diverging from the health contract
A consumer outside compute_validity (e.g., a renderer calling manifest_pins() directly) does not have a catch boundary specified and raises to the caller

findings.finding: The `profile` parameter added to `compute_validity` has no enumerated value vocabulary. The plan references 'claim/default profile', 'close profile', and 'drift-refresh/default profile' as informal prose labels but never defines the formal string or enum values an implementer must pass for `profile=`. A code author cannot determine whether to write `profile='close'`, `profile='close-profile'`, `profile=Profile.CLOSE`, or something else.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Extend the existing attest.compute_validity(attestation, ticket_state, kind, *, repo_root=None) with an optional profile parameter'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Implementation section a table such as: `profile=None` (default/claim): all checks; `profile='close'`: signature + registry + own-material + configured-pins, `check_code_freshness=False`; clarify whether 'drift-refresh' is a distinct value or an alias for the default.

findings.finding: The mechanism for AC 'Enabled pin drift prevents progressive drift refresh' is unspecified: `drift_refresh_candidate` (attest.py:146-179) currently returns a non-None candidate based only on material+regver checks — it never checks pin health. Under the new plan, if enforcement is enabled and pins have drifted, `drift_refresh_candidate` would still return a valid candidate and `drift_refresh` would proceed to the probe, violating the AC. The plan does not say whether `drift_refresh_candidate` itself should call `compute_plan_review_validity` before returning, whether `drift_refresh` should call it as a pre-gate, or whether the profile check is threaded through the candidate return value.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'drift refresh' consumer / AC: 'Enabled pin drift prevents progressive drift refresh'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence: 'Before returning a refreshable candidate, `drift_refresh_candidate` calls `compute_plan_review_validity` with the drift-refresh profile (enforcement check enabled); a non-current pin_status causes it to return None (→ full review), consistent with how material/regver mismatches already block the candidate.'

findings.finding: The processing rule connecting `DerivedPlanReviewHealth.pin_status != current` to `drift_refresh_candidate` returning `None` is never formally specified. The plan says 'enabled non-current pin health returns None and forces a full review before any probe', but 'non-current pin health' is informal shorthand — it is never linked to the `DerivedPlanReviewHealth.pin_status` field or any specific subset of its values (stale-pin-drift vs stale-pin-missing vs malformed-pin).
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Before returning a refresh candidate, drift_refresh_candidate calls compute_validity with the drift-refresh/default profile; enabled non-current pin health returns None and forces a full review before any probe.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Broaden the six per-consumer 'Enabled pin drift invalidates …' ACs to cover all three invalidating pin statuses (stale-pin-drift, stale-pin-missing, malformed-pin), or add explicit companion ACs for malformed and missing cases at each consumer.
evidence: Implementation: 'When enabled, drift, malformed pins, or a deleted/missing pinned target invalidate the attestation.'
AC: 'Enabled pin drift invalidates the claim consumer.' (and five analogous ACs for other consumers)
AC: 'Missing/deleted, archived, malformed, duplicate, and re-enable scenarios are deterministic.' — covers determinism, not per-consumer valid=false for each consumer.
findings.finding: The per-consumer invalidation ACs name only 'drift' (stale-pin-drift) but the Implementation specifies that `stale-pin-missing` and `malformed-pin` also produce `valid=false` when enforcement is enabled. The only coverage for malformed/missing across individual consumers is the catch-all 'Missing/deleted, archived, malformed, duplicate, and re-enable scenarios are deterministic,' which tests determinism rather than per-consumer invalidation at claim_gate_check, idempotent reuse, drift_refresh, re-sign, audit, and derived health.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — per-consumer ACs ('Enabled pin drift invalidates the claim consumer', etc.) vs. Implementation ('drift, malformed pins, or a deleted/missing pinned target invalidate the attestation')
scenarios: Malformed-pin or stale-pin-missing scenarios pass verification at an individual consumer (e.g., idempotent reuse) but are not caught because the AC for that consumer is scoped only to drift; the determinism catch-all does not require those scenarios to fail at each consumer.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Break 'Missing/deleted, archived, malformed, duplicate, and re-enable scenarios are deterministic' into per-scenario ACs each stating the expected pin_status and valid value (e.g., 'deleted pinned target with enforcement enabled yields pin_status=stale-pin-missing and valid=false'; define what 'duplicate' means and its expected outcome).
evidence: 'Missing/deleted, archived, malformed, duplicate, and re-enable scenarios are deterministic' — no per-scenario expected output stated
The Implementation section describes behavior for 'drift, malformed pins, or a deleted/missing pinned target' and 'archived targets' separately, but the AC collapses all into one vague assertion
'duplicate' is not defined anywhere in the Implementation section — an executor cannot determine what 'duplicate' means or what the expected pin_status/valid result should be
findings.finding: The AC 'Missing/deleted, archived, malformed, duplicate, and re-enable scenarios are deterministic' bundles at least five distinct sub-scenarios into one criterion without stating a specific observable outcome for any of them. 'Deterministic' is not a measurable end-state — it does not specify what valid/pin_status value is expected for each sub-scenario (e.g., deleted pinned target → stale-pin-missing + valid=false when enforced; duplicate target IDs → malformed-pin; re-enable after disable → no pin rewrite). An executor cannot confirm this AC without knowing what 'done' looks like per sub-scenario.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Missing/deleted, archived, malformed, duplicate, and re-enable scenarios are deterministic'
scenarios: Executor cannot write a falsifiable test for 'duplicate' without a definition
Executor cannot distinguish 're-enable' behavior (no pin rewrite stated in AC, only in Implementation) as a distinct verifiable outcome
An ambiguous 'deterministic' criterion can pass vacuously if sub-scenarios are never individually exercised
findings.suggested_fix: Replace with: (a) 'Deleted/missing pinned target with enforcement enabled → pin_status=stale-pin-missing, valid=false'; (b) 'Archived target is readable and yields stale-pin-drift or current per fingerprint comparison'; (c) 'Malformed manifest metadata → pin_status=malformed-pin, valid=false when enforced, advisory when disabled'; (d) define 'duplicate' (e.g., two targets with the same canonical_id) → 'duplicate target canonical_id in manifest → pin_status=malformed-pin'; (e) 're-enable enforcement after disable → no existing pins are rewritten, pin_status reflects live comparison'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the verdict string values emitted by compute_validity for pin-enforcement failures (e.g., 'stale-pin-drift', 'stale-pin-missing', 'malformed-pin') co-located with the DerivedPlanReviewHealth and compute_validity extension spec.
evidence: Existing verdict values at attest.py:499, 513, 565, 585, 598, 619, 624 — established string vocabulary
Plan: 'all renderers consume this contract rather than duplicating strings' — implies verdict strings are canonical, but does not define the new ones
ACs reference 'enabled pin drift' making valid=false but do not state the verdict string value emitted in that case
Audit consumers and the close-gate branch on verdict to determine reason; an undefined verdict for pin failures means they cannot distinguish a pin failure from a code-drift failure
findings.finding: The plan adds a 'health' key beside compute_validity's existing {valid, reason, verdict} return, but nowhere specifies the new verdict string values to emit for the pin-status failure modes when enforcement is enabled. Existing verdict values (stale-code, stale-material, stale-regver, stale-head, certified, unsigned) are established in the codebase; the plan does not define new verdict strings for pin failures (e.g., 'stale-pin-drift', 'stale-pin-missing', 'malformed-pin') that consumers branch on for audit, rendering, and gate routing.
findings.location: Implementation section, compute_validity extension; Acceptance Criteria 'Audit reports enabled pin drift as invalid'
scenarios: Audit renderer displays 'stale' as a generic fallback instead of a pin-specific message because the verdict string is implementation-defined
A downstream gate branches on verdict=='stale-code' to decide whether to allow close, misidentifying a pin failure as a code-drift failure
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Implementation: 'When enforcement is enabled and pin_status is stale-pin-drift, stale-pin-missing, or malformed-pin, compute_validity returns verdict="<pin_status_value>" (e.g., verdict="stale-pin-drift"). When enforcement is disabled, valid remains true and verdict remains "certified"; the health struct carries the advisory pin_status.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
